sentences.text: Mongolian-Russian soldiers.
Yeah.
Before the Russian arrived to Birkenau, one day, the Germans, they came, and they know that all these Russian soldiers could make a lot of trouble.
They could even have a fight with the Germans.
They took all of them, two or three days before the Russian arrived to Auschwitz.
They was putting them next to the wall.
They killed them all.
Not one Russian soldier stayed alive.
They killed them all.
[INAUDIBLE] two or three times.
Twice I had to go there, because she didn't feel good, and she needed some medicine.
And I came to this hospital.
And one of the doctor-- Jewish doctor woman-- she told me what medicine I should bring.
And I had all the connection with the doctor, so I brought the medicine.
I brought her vitamins.
I brought her clothing.
I could do everything, because I had the opportunity, you understand?

In fact, before, we knew already two or three days before that we're leaving Birkenau, Auschwitz, because we had to move away, an old Polish friends-- they liked me very much because I talked the Hebrew language, and they liked to be with Jews who knows Hebrew-- and they told me, listen, now we have to go away from here, and try to move away from here, because when they made empty camp, they kill everybody.
Nobody will stay alive.
So I figured, I have to go away.
And this was the middle of the night I got the information.
And I knew my sister is next door.
I couldn't wake her up.
I couldn't go to next door, so I got, right away, boots, pants, coats, everything.
I had the connection there.
And I start middle of the night, two o'clock, with stones to knock in the barrack.
Somebody heard this knocking.
They came out.
I said, listen, I give you a nice present, just send me out my sister.
I want to talk to her just for one minute.
And I give her all the package, and I told her, listen, from here, we have to go away because everybody could be destroyed, will be killed.
So here, you have clothing, and see to it that you should also go.
Of course, her luck was that she did go, because she couldn't survive.
But I survived.
She was sick also this time.
And one of the organizer, they took all the twins, and was hiding at somewhere in that place.
I don't know how, but they was hidden.
And was hiding them until the Russian came.
And she survived there in January.
If it was January 17 or 18, I don't know.
But I left from Birkenau.
Yeah, we knew when it was a holiday.
How did you know?
There were very religious people there, and they knew exactly, and they reminded us, all the people, that today is Rosh Hashanah.
And then, let me tell you, they didn't have to remind us.
The Germans reminded us, because each time came a Jewish holiday, then was the selection.
So they didn't even have to remind us.
Smaller, smaller holidays, like [HEBREW], that maybe the German didn't know about it, they found out from those people.
But those main-- Rosh Hashana and the Yom Kippur-- was always a selection.
Always.
I mean, always, I was only one Rosh Hashana, one Yom Kippur.
Right.
Were you able to observe any religious--
I have my own [INAUDIBLE].
In fact, I brought home one of the [INAUDIBLE].
I had one.
I still have at home.
Yeah.
But mostly, was very hard.
They took everything away.
Yeah, yeah.
Because I was in a barrack, I had my privacy, so, yeah, I could.
Yeah.
No, no.
This I don't think's a-- at least I didn't fast.
I didn't think about it like this.
Yeah, but maybe there are some, maybe very religious.
Happened to her once settlement came.
She was a [NON-ENGLISH], a rabbi, survived, a young woman, 22 year old.
And when they told them, I was there standing, and they told them everybody has to lay down on the knee.
And [INAUDIBLE] but the Jewish people to lay down on the knees against the Jewish law, except to certain holidays we do this.
And the SS said, lay down.
She didn't want.
She said, no, I'm not doing it.
I wouldn't do it.
It's against my religion.
He with his boot gave her once in her stomach and twice in the stomach, so she grabbed it on the spot.
Yeah, my kapo, for the twins, an older person, who was approximate, those days, he could be around 65 year old.
He was sent to-- he had a truck store in Germany.
And because they find out he's homosexual, he was sent-- most of the homosexual were sent to concentration camps.
Yeah.
Maybe [INAUDIBLE] himself was one.
And he knew that is wrong, so he sent-- instead of to sent himself to concentration, he was sending the rest.
No, I don't remember.
I think Wolf, Wolf, something like Wolf.
But I'm not sure in it.
He took care on the children.
He-- whenever was a little left over, because he's the one from the barrack who was dividing the food.
Maybe he was so kind and took off a little more from the older people in order to leftover more for the children.
He used to look for the children should be covered up with a covering, evening, when they sleeping.
In the morning, when they used to wake up, people used to take a stick and knock the beds.
[GERMAN]!
And then told us slowly with kindness, with feeling, to wake up the children.
He had feeling.
Yeah.
This Wolf?
Hey, if you got a job to be a kapo, you know what's a kapo means?
He had a president couldn't have such a good job like in Auschwitz to be a kapo.
They knew it.
They knew by his-- every German who was in, in a camp was for two reasons, or because of politic, or because he was a, a homosexual.
Identification, we had only where we have-- each had a number.
There was a triangle for different color.
A Russian guy had a black.
A Jew, if I'm not mistaken, was yellow.
A Polish I think was a-- a Polish was red, I think.
A Jew was yellow.
Something.
When-- when they looked at you, whoever it is had need your number, they saw right away the number plus who you are, from what country.
Everything was organized.
I wouldn't be able to tell you, no.
But they knew.
There was talking about it.
No, I didn't see nurse.
And in fact, in my barrack was no woman at all.
Just men.
But they asked my doctor-- we was very friendly, because came mid-evening, he had to tell, he told me about his family, and I [INAUDIBLE] about my family.
So one day I said, listen, doctor.
I never saw in my life yet an operation.
Would you allow me to see when you make an operation?
He said, why not?
Tomorrow, if you want, come in, 10 o'clock.
And we make an operation.
I said, what kind of operation?
He says, one of the guy has in the back for his rib.
There is water in the rib.
So I figured, maybe we could give him an injection and do something.
He sat down on a chair, like I, me, the way I'm sitting now.
Two guys was holding his hand.
Can you imagine, like this?
And the guy went with a knife and started to cut out one the middle rib.
The guy was screaming.
The [INAUDIBLE] unbelievable.
Can you imagine the pain?
And they did it like we're drinking a coffee now.
Now that you mention about operation, I have to tell you something.
Everything was by them engineerly figured.
Now let's say you made a selection.
As I told you before, the crematorium had to work 24 hours.
So they couldn't do it there today, get customers for the crematorium, or get patients for the crematorium.
We had to prepare it weeks and months before.
So when they knew already we have to prepare another 200, 500 people, they came to the camp.
Only Jews, of course.
No other ones.
[INAUDIBLE] and they [INAUDIBLE] everybody to stand in line, undress completely, in the open field.
And whoever is skinny, he is sick, he is weak, knocked out.
Now let's say this happened, this selection, let's say the selection happened, let's say, Thursday.
And next week, Monday, came the truck and had to take him away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the guy was also marked down for a week before to make, have an operation.
There was no such a thing you shouldn't have the operation, this sadistic operation.
The operation had to be done, even if you are already on the list to be next week to take crematorium.
The operation had to be done.
[GERMAN] Order has to be.
That was the philosophy, the German intelligent philosophy.
So let him go already, he has to die already.
But they have to make him an operation.
Cut him.
At least he's alive.
No.
Was such a thing.
They did it.
We had a French, in Auschwitz.
I have to tell you.
A French general.
The guy could be [INAUDIBLE], maybe 70 year old.
And intelligent, a fine person.
A real gentleman.
I don't know how they found out.
He was first between the regular soldiers who was in captivity there.
And how they found out, they found out he's Jewish.
And came the selection, and they marked down his number, and they took him away.
You know, I was looking in his eyes, and I could not understand how a human being-- intelligent, a general-- they used to give him respect because they knew he's a general.
And the truck came, and he knew has to go, and he knew where he's going.
He went to every child to [INAUDIBLE], shake hand.
He did smile, thank you.
So nice.
You treated me so good.
And I said, how, how can do this, a human being?
They take you tomorrow, or right away, and he's still a gentleman.
I will-- I will never forget his face, the way he behaved.
Do you recall his name, by any chance?
No, [INAUDIBLE].
A French general.
That's what he-- every knew about this, the French general.
Last week at Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before we tell you-- before I'll tell you about Auschwitz, I have a very important things to tell you.
The Sonderkommando, what I told you, had a connection with an SS, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that this particular SS will get a bottle of diamonds, [INAUDIBLE] just say a bottle of diamonds.
This means because the Sonderkommando had in the hands when they-- things they had it in the hand, all kind of stuff, and diamond was the smallest thing to have in a small place hidden.
And they told him you get this particular, probably millions involved, but you have to give us rubber gloves, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] heavy-duty scissors, and guns.

If it comes to a point when the six months is over-- because they knew after six months, they'd be destroyed, they should try to cut the wire, try to kill somebody, try to run, and nothing to lose anyway.
He knew he has to be destroyed anyway.
But the kapo, who was a Jewish, Polish Jew, when they-- every six months they changed the Sonderkommando, they didn't changed him.
The German trusted him so much, they let them alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this son of a bitch, to say, he found out about the plan.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he reported this to the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was only four months.
This could leave another two months.
And this was, I would say, between September, October.
I cannot tell you exactly.
I didn't have diary to mark down everything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They already felt, they knew that they found out about the plan.
And there was four crematoriums.
They were connected with telephone wire, the four, underground, organized.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] destroyed the crematorium.
They had stuff to blow it up.
And they started to take a fight with the Germans, because they knew what is waiting for them.
Now, this particular place was in Birkenau.
Birkenau, between Birkenau and Auschwitz was 12 kilometer.
And by the time was not enough SS to take care of these guys.
By the time they came from Auschwitz, help SS for this SS in Birkenau, they killed 22 SS.
Half of them got killed outside from the-- those 500 people.
And they say about one third, or half, was in the burning crematorium.
They were singing 'Hatikvah'.
Was a Thursday afternoon, even the sky was bringing them together.
You know, it's a sad day, but I will never forget in my life.
They say that one or two guys run away.
I cannot believe.
The way Birkenau was organized, wires and wires.
But they say yes.
I don't know.
I couldn't tell you if this is true or not.
So that's what happen.
Now, what happened later, later on came [INAUDIBLE] transport came.
And was no more crematorium.
And from this day, this date on, until January 17, all the older people who arrived at the transport, they stayed alive.
That's what they accomplished.
They didn't accomplish days to survive, but they accomplished a nice few hundred people who-- older people who survived.

It also could be that the German special let them alive because when the Russia now coming and the world should see that they left older people alive, this could be also because, I mean, they could destroy them in a different way.
They destroyed all the Russian soldiers.
None of them was left.
So this was the last-minute excitement before I left Birkenau.
Mengele, I would say, I saw him maybe a month before I left Auschwitz.
Roughly speaking, yeah.
Some people-- some of the guys, they told me that the main thing what he was concentrated on the papers, when he packed in his office, about the twins, all the papers.
When I arrived already to Mauthausen, I did not mention what twin, because I was afraid that they would continue their experiment.
I already knew what is waiting for us if he would continue those experiment.
Auschwitz, we knew we have to go.
And I got myself a very good suit, and I got myself dressed up, because it was January, very cold.
And I had a small feet, but I couldn't get for my feet a pair of shoes.
So I got myself a big shoe, what is with three numbers bigger, and I took all kind of shmatas.
And I started a stick, and we had to walk.
I think we were walking, from Birkenau, about two days.
Just walking.
We arrived to a town where trains were waiting for us.
If the name of the town was Leslow or Breslow, I don't know.
But there were waiting trains, open trains.
God forbid not closed trains.
Completely open trains.
And there they was putting us on, and took us, roughly speaking, I would say, two days to arrive to Mauthausen.
Destination?
To the hell.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we arrived to Mauthausen.
Mauthausen was located on a big hill.
And we get out from the train.
We had to get to Mauthausen camp.
Hundreds of steps.
I don't know how long it took us to climb up there.
Steps, hundreds of steps!
We arrived approximately-- yeah.
From this train, I would say half of them was dead already, because snow was falling us.
No food.
Here a dead body on this side, and here a dead body on this side.
[INAUDIBLE] was cold.
And the only thing that we could eat in these two days on the train, there was a special dish, they called it [NON-ENGLISH].
It's a little aluminum oval dish.
It has a wire to hold it.
And everybody took off from his shirt a piece made of string, and was connecting it to this particular dish.
And while this train was running, we picked up snow.
This was the food what we-- this was our food.
We arriving to Mauthausen approximately around 11 o'clock in the evening.
Right away, they bring us in into a shower.
This is the first thing by the Germans.
[GERMAN], we have to be clean.
And they give us hot shower.
They didn't ask if it's too hot or too cold.
Hot like hell.
After the shower, you went through a selection.
The selection was all done in a different style like in Auschwitz.
One guy was standing with a black pencil, and any person who passed by and he saw he's not anymore ready for work, he just made an X on his back, and this guy was already-- they didn't use their gas.
They used-- they electrocuted. 12 o'clock, we were finished with the shower.
They threw us outside on the snow naked and till morning, 6 o'clock, after a hot shower, hundreds there, or thousands, I don't know how many.
Probably a thousand there, sitting-- standing, not sitting.
There was-- you couldn't sit on the snow.
Naked, after a hot shower.
I don't even know if somebody got to catch a cold.
I didn't see nobody to sneeze there.
Maybe we didn't have even [INAUDIBLE] to sneeze. 6 o'clock in the morning comes the kapo.
Over there there were Spanish kapos.
If they were Jews or non-Jews, I don't know.
They was hitting and beating without the-- nervous, and screaming, Du bist nicht in Auschwitz!
Du bist in Mauthausen!
It means, you are not anymore in Auschwitz!
You are in Mauthausen!
What the different where I am?
Here is a hell and over there is a hell.
Beating for no reason whatsoever.
And taking us to a big barrack, a barrack what is, like, half a block, a big barrack.
And puts everything had to lay, everybody had to lay down on a floor.
Head, feet, head, feet, head, feet.
And came the kapo.
And covering us up, all of them, with a blanket.
And he was walking on us.
Walking!
One guy, he broke the nose, and one guy, he took out the eyes, and was terrible.
And you couldn't see nothing.
If you [INAUDIBLE], he came, he was dancing on you.
So whatever happened to you, be quiet.
That's how it is.
That's your life, and that's how you have to take it.
So next day, they gave us a soup.
And came Sunday, louse controller.
Louse controller means, you have to check.
You have to be undressed.
Just take with you your shirt, and see if you haven't got lice in your shirt.
But he didn't look the lice if you have it here or here.
That's how he looked.
And if he find a little, in the side, an egg from a lice, they was beating you.
This pains what you had, you just couldn't take it anymore.
This was his pleasure.
So I figured, oh, no.
In this-- in this place, I cannot stay.
And whenever will be a first transport, they say, here they go a transport, there goes a transport, I said, whenever there's a transport first, I want to get out from here.
Because this is not a place-- here they will kill me.
And they said, there is a transport who goes to Melk.
I don't know how many kilometer was this Melk.
All I know, they took it with a train, and we arriving to a-- Melk.
Melk, we arrive, evening, could be about 5 o'clock.
And there is the big, big camp.
And people are going every day from this camp for a job.
Right away they told us, is not so easy here.
With wooden, wooden shoes, you have to go every day, 10 kilometer to the job, and 10 kilometer back from the job.
I figure I wouldn't be able to.
And here, what I had trouble with my feet, you know, from all the wetness, and from all this, I got the green stuff on my feet.
I cannot walk with it anymore.
I'm sick.
So it was-- they were there, Polish people, non-Jews, because of politic, whatever, they was against the German government.
And they took the whole families to camps, and between those people were older people and children, children 10 year old, 12 year old, 15 year old.
And they was-- they took about 200 children to go in a kitchen to peel potatoes for the SS.
The potato has to be so clean.
If god forbid would be a point, you're finished.
And I was a little taller than the average children, but I figure myself, it's a little already close to evening.
I stayed on my knee, and I had to went to this transport to be there between that.
The regular job that I would go 10 kilometer and wouldn't survive, let me take this chance.
And I got into these kids, between these kids.
And I got to this kitchen.
They called it [GERMAN].
Was peeling potatoes.
